chunk_summary: The core insight: Even AI giants like Anthropic and OpenAI are hiring hundreds of experienced human marketers at $200K-$400K salaries. Why? Because AI alone cannot make the strategic decisions around positioning, segmentation, and differentiation. AI gives clean answers, but changes completely with new information. The right way to use AI for marketing is with human judgment directing it — the 'human-AI loop' — not trying to one-shot strategy from a prompt.
content: I was trading emails with the founder. He said to me, TK. I've got Mackenzie as my marketing strategist and I've got Wilson Santini on my legal side. He, of course, was not talking about the actual firm, Mackenzie or Wilson Santini. He was talking about his laptop himself and his cloud code instance. I saw something else at the same time. Anthropic. They have over 22 open positions just in their San Francisco office. Hiring for marketers. OpenAI also has four product marketing positions, paying upwards of $300,000 for marketing. Here's a big question. What are these AI companies know that this founder doesn't, and that maybe I don't and you don't. It turns out that there is a right way and a wrong way to use AI for marketing today. ... Now, you could go into cloud code or ChatGPT or Gemini and say, hey, this is the product that I sell. This is the target customer that I want to sell to. Please figure out my positioning, segmentation, and differentiation. I can turn on deep research and you can run it, but you've probably experienced this more than once. Where it will come back with a very neat and clean answer. ... And then you can actually give it some unique new piece of information. You can say, well, actually, I forgot to mention, this is one of the key things that really differentiates us. Now, almost immediately, it will come back with a completely different answer. This is a conundrum that exists today. ... even given that they're paying hundreds of thousands of dollars to real marketers to work with the AI, to actually figure out what the ideal customer profile is, what the positioning is, what the segmentation is, what the competitive differentiation is. This doesn't mean that you don't use AI. They're still going to use AI. They're probably going to use an even more powerful version of AI than we have access to in the public. But at the same time, they're hiring experienced people and paying them anywhere from $200,000 to $320,000 to actually work with the AI to make distinctions around this.
questions_on_this_chunk: Why are AI companies hiring human marketers?
Can I use ChatGPT to figure out my SaaS positioning?
What is the right way to use AI for marketing strategy?
Why does AI give different answers when I add new information?

chunk_summary: Principle 1 — Ideal Customer Profile (ICP): product marketing's most important job. Go beyond superficial firmographics to truly define positioning, segmentation, and competitive differentiation. The competition isn't just other software — it's Excel spreadsheets and apathy. OpenAI is hiring dedicated product marketers per product line because each ICP needs its own strategy.
content: Principle number one. the first principle is to actually build out in your marketing strategy, ideal customer profile. Now, if you actually look a little bit closer to that OpenAI listing that I showed, they listed for four product marketing positions for each of their product lines. And a lot of times when it comes to marketing, people conflate the different disciplines of marketing that exists. There's three disciplines of marketing. The first discipline is product marketing. The second is brand marketing, and the third is demand generation marketing. Now, brand marketing comes much later for companies that says when you talk about the colors and the logo and the brand, no one actually cares because no one knows you. But product marketing and demand generation marketing are the two core pieces for startups to succeed, and OpenAI is hiring product marketers. One of the biggest things that product marketers figure out is the ideal customer profile. ... So the first principle that really goes into building out your marketing strategy will work in 2026 is defining your ideal customer profiles. When done in the right way. There are few key decisions that you'll make. The first one is your positioning. The second is your segmentation. And the third is your competitive differentiation. ... It's not just about saying, oh, we only want to target companies that have 50 to 100 employees in North America that are in manufacturing. I just made that one up that maybe you're ICP. It's not just about that, but it's digging deeper and really understanding what is our positioning compared to the others in the market. How do we segment the market and focus on a specific part? How do we actually differentiate ourselves and actually position ourselves against the competition, given all the factors that are out there? And a lot of times the competition is not just another software provider, it could just be an Excel spreadsheet, or it could be doing Apathy, which is the greatest competitor software even for AI companies today.
questions_on_this_chunk: What are the three disciplines of marketing?
What does an ideal customer profile really include?
Why is apathy the biggest competitor for SaaS?
How does positioning differ from segmentation?

chunk_summary: Principle 2 — The Manifesto (strategic narrative): based on your ICP, define your value proposition (one-sentence explanation of what you do, the problem you solve, and why you're 10x better) and your messaging (how that value prop is deployed across homepage, cold emails, ads, lead magnets). Anthropic is hiring a Head of GTM Narrative at $320K-$400K+ because they need human judgment to differentiate from OpenAI, address fears, and educate the market. AI can't make these strategic calls.
content: Now the second principle, as you're figuring out and deploying your marketing strategy ... The second piece that really comes into play is what I call the manifesto. ... Some people think that the manifesto is you getting on a soapbox and putting a tab on your website saying manifesto and just talking at people about why you're the best. That's not what a manifesto is. There's some specific settings that go into building a proper manifesto. Another name for this is your strategic narrative. Now, based on your positioning, your segmentation, your competitive differentiation, there's a few things that you have to define. The first, most simplest thing, especially when you're in the early stages, if you're trying to go from, say, zero till your first ten K, remember are the core basic theme that you really need to define is your value proposition. This is exactly how you explain what you do, why you're ten times better, and what the problem is. So in a very succinct way, in a one sentence kind of way, and this will feed into whatever cold email ads, organic, social, whatever you want to actually do in terms of marketing execution. The second thing is your strategic narrative. And it's your core messaging. If the value proposition is your one sentence explanation of what you do, what problem you solve for your four, and why you're ten times better, the messaging is how that value proposition is actually deployed across key assets on your marketing. This could include your homepage, which I know you're changing because you're still figuring it out. Or your outbound email in a subject line or an ad or a lead magnet, you name it, the messaging is going to determine what all those things are. And so determining your value proposition and your messaging is an exercise you have to go through. This is a great example of another job post I found for anthropic. Anthropic is actually hiring for ahead of go to market narrative, And they're paying anywhere between 300 and $20,000 to over $400,000 for this role. And when you dig into that job description, they're basically asking people to say, hey, we need you to figure out our broader message, our strategic narrative. We need you to figure out our messaging on how to actually differentiate ourselves from the obvious competitor, open AI. We need to actually educate people about the common objections and fears that they have about our brand, so that we can actually deploy this messaging out into all of our marketing and sales motions. That's how important this piece is. And it's so important that I can't just solve it. ... it will always give you an answer. It will never tell you, 'Hey, I don't know the answer.' It will never tell you, 'Hey, we've got some real headwinds, and we don't know how to message against that.' ... Which is why even entropic and open AI is paying hundreds and thousands of dollars to actually have humans use the AI to make these decisions and talk through them and make the right calls.
questions_on_this_chunk: What is a manifesto in SaaS marketing?
What's the difference between value proposition and messaging?
Why is Anthropic hiring a Head of GTM Narrative?
Can AI write my strategic narrative for me?

chunk_summary: One ICP rule: you cannot effectively go after multiple ICPs unless you have separate budgets, staffing, and strategies for each. OpenAI hires separate product marketers per ICP. If you're resource-constrained, conquer one ICP first, then expand. Each ICP requires its own manifesto and its own execution motion.
content: A lot of times, one of the biggest pitfalls that I see what founders are at the stage is they try to go after two ISPs. And I've seen this with founders at ten K, and there are upwards of founders at 5 million or 10 million in ARR. And the problem is you can't go after multiple ISPs. However, you need to actually be staffed appropriately and budgeted appropriately to separate out the areas of focus and execute against those target ISPs. So take the open AI job description again, for example. They're hiring a product marketing person for different areas for different ISPs. That's how you have to do it. You try to get one person and one AI to just figure all this out. You'll be neither here nor there. So you always have to decide, well, do I want to take these multiple ISPs and do I have enough attention, time, budget and resources to go after it? Or am I better off actually focusing on one ISP, better off to first conquer this ICP and then expand from there, which essentially is going to really depend on your budget, your resources, your time, your team size, and your attention span. So you'll have to figure out if you can go after multiple ISPs, because for each ICP you go after, you all have to have a different manifesto and a different motion that you actually execute on.
questions_on_this_chunk: Can I target multiple ICPs at the same time?
How does OpenAI structure marketing for multiple products?
What happens if I try to go after two ICPs with one strategy?
When should I expand to a second ICP?

chunk_summary: Principle 3 — The Broadway Show: once you have your ICP and manifesto, execute consistently in 3-week sprints instead of scatterbrained channel-hopping. The execution stack that works in 2026: 1) Organic social (test ICP and messaging), 2) Paid social (scale what worked organically), 3) Outbound (with dual-pronged awareness from paid social), 4) SEM, SEO, and events (longer-tail, added later). Optimize for pipeline and revenue, not likes. Marketing is 1-to-many and infinitely scalable; sales is 1-to-1. Better marketing makes sales easier because trust is pre-built.
content: Number three. Number three is once you have these two things, you essentially have your go to market and marketing strategy. And don't let the word strategy confuse you. It's not fancy, it's just choices. You made choices here. And only you can make these choices. Once you've done that, then comes the third pillar, the third principle, which is your Broadway. I call this the Broadway show. ... The reason I call this the Broadway show is one of the biggest mistakes I've seen early stage companies make is they get scatterbrained. They're like, oh, they're doing TikTok yesterday, LinkedIn today, Twitter tomorrow, ICP today, ICP tomorrow, years pass by and they're like, we don't know what's going on. We don't know why we can break through. The reason this happens is you're scatterbrained and marketing only works when you run in three week iterations. You running for three weeks, you collect the data, you look at the insights, you iterate. If you get scatterbrained, then you will never get enough data to really understand whether your manifesto and your ICP is working. So I call this a Broadway show. You create it once and you run it for a three week period, and if it works well, then you scale it. If it doesn't work, then you iterate on it and you run it again, and then you scale it. ... there's a few things that go into the Broadway show, for every single time I work on a company and getting the marketing strategies going, I follow a very specific set of tactics. And this is what's working in 2026. Today, the first thing that we do is organic social. So we take our messaging, we take our manifesto, and we turn it into an actual, asset. Our lead magnet that we can use. And we take the messaging and we start deploying in an organic social. And our core metric for this is pipeline and then revenue. That's the core metric. So anything that we do has a yield in pipeline and revenue. ... So the first place we test this, the ICP and the manifesto is organic social based on the performance of organic social. Then we move it over to paid social. So if it worked in organic it's going to work in paid. So we do this for three weeks. If it works then we convert to paid social. We pay money. That way we don't have to become a content creator or an influencer. That's not the job that you're in. ... As we get paid social going. Then we turn on outbound because we know this messaging is working. We also do it in a way where if they look at our outbound, they'll see our paid social. So there's a dual flagged attack, and then we turn on, additional sources like SEM, SEO and even events. ... So that's how we run a Broadway show. We run a Broadway show where we're optimizing for pipeline and revenues. We start with organic, social. We then do paid social, then outbound, then the other pieces like SEM, SEO and events. So you can kind of scale it out that way. As you start to gain momentum and each of these become more and more scalable. So it kind of starts unscalable and then you becomes more and more scalable. ... the difference between marketing versus sales and the relationship between them, go to market is marketing plus sales. Marketing is one to many. So this is one to many and sales is 1 to 1. ... marketing is infinitely scalable. Once you get your Broadway show working and you got paid social going and it's converting to real meetings and pipeline and trials, you can infinitely scale that. ... sales is a 1 to 1 game. You need to hire more people to reach more people, more salespeople, more SDRs. ... The other thing a lot of people don't realize on why this is so powerful is the better the marketing, the easier the sale. Because if the first time someone's hearing about is through a cold email and then they book a call, they know nothing about you. There is no trust. But if they're coming through this entire marketing motion, then they know you. They trust you more. They've been educated by you. They've been nurtured by you. By the time you get to sales, the sale is that much easier because a lot of that trust has been built.
questions_on_this_chunk: What is the Broadway Show in SaaS marketing?
Why should I start with organic social before paid?
What order should I activate marketing channels?
Why does better marketing make sales easier?

chunk_summary: Real case studies with metrics: four examples of companies running the ICP → Manifesto → Broadway Show framework. Example metrics: LinkedIn organic converting at 43-49%, paid social at 14-22%. Traffic quality (ICP lead %) tracked to train ad algorithms. One company generated 616 leads with 19% conversion and 52% traffic quality. Another saw 80% traffic quality from LinkedIn paid. All use a dashboard to track traffic → conversion → activation by channel weekly, then iterate. The key is 3-week cycles of data, insights, and scaling.
content: I promise you that not only would I show you the actual principles and the actual tactics that would bring receipts, so let's actually dig into some of the receipts, because every single time I work with someone, I work with a founder going through my go to market program, we first at ICP size, then we do the manifesto and then we run the Broadway show. And as a Broadway show works. We scale it out. So the reason I know these three pieces work is because I do it all day and every day with founders. So what I want to show you is the exact metrics we monitor for and how we scale. ... So for this particular one, this is an actual dashboard of the tool that we use to run these Broadway shows. It's called instead. And it shows you traffic conversion and activation. Traffic is how much attention we're getting on a day to day basis. Conversion is how many leads that we're getting, and activation is how many of those leads are taking the next step in the process, actually activating to look at booking a call or starting a trial, whatever it may be. ... So for this particular one, you'll notice that they generated about 129 leads and 53% of them took, activate. It took that next step to look at, whether to book a call or to start a trial. ... LinkedIn organic. If you're in B2B, SaaS and AI LinkedIn organic is one of the best. And so in this particular, inbound funnel we started with LinkedIn organic. ... you'll notice that LinkedIn organic has a 43% conversion rate paid has a 14% conversion rate for paid. That's pretty good. ... The second place where this particular funnel is getting a lot of leads, is the corporate website. When people go to your corporate website, only 10% of people on average actually take the next step, whether it's booking a call or starting a trial, the rest just leave. So in this case, 42% of people are actually taking the lead magnet instead of just leaving, which is really great. ... Let's go to the next one. This one's slightly different because every ICP is different. ... they have generated 616 leads. So that's over here. They're seeing a 19% conversion rate. And 27% of them are getting to the next step. And their primary one is Facebook. They figure it out very quickly. Like, look, our ICP is not on LinkedIn, but they are on Facebook and they are on Instagram, which is great. ... one of the things that they did is they launched it. And with Facebook, organic is a little bit tougher. You got to jump to page, but there's some ways to test it. So eventually they start scaling with Facebook paid. ... One thing to keep in mind, and we'll show this in the next one, is we also measure traffic quality. So when we get a lead we kind of have them self-identify to understand is this an ICP lead or not an ICP lead? ... So in this case, you'll notice that they have a 52% traffic quality, meaning half the leads are ICP leads. ... Now let me show you the third one. ... 982 visits, they have generated 201 leads, 21% conversion rate. This is super interesting. 49% conversion rate on LinkedIn, organic. But this particular funnel, they're not posting organically anymore. They're just not. It's just all ads. They've taken their best organic posts, turn it into paid. It just cranks 6% activation rate. ... they're getting 80% traffic quality. ... And the last one I'll show you, over here. ... LinkedIn is the primary channel 495. Visit 159 leads LinkedIn organic just crushing it paid is turned on 74% traffic quality 15% activation.
questions_on_this_chunk: What metrics should I track for SaaS marketing?
What is a good conversion rate on LinkedIn organic?
How do I use traffic quality to improve ad targeting?
What does a Broadway Show dashboard look like?
